projects.name: Project 1 — Dungeon Walk
projects.goal: Build a terminal-rendered, Doom-style 3D dungeon that loads maps and lets the player walk through rooms and corridors.
projects.final_product: A tested terminal raycaster with map loading, movement, collision, doors, simple entities, save/load, and release documentation.

chapters.name: Create the Alire Workspace
chapters.concept: Alire crates and build workflow
chapters.deliverable: Create the dungeon_walk crate, understand alire.toml, build it, run it, and locate generated artifacts.

chapters.name: Terminal Output and Player Commands
chapters.concept: Ada.Text_IO and basic input
chapters.deliverable: Print a title screen, prompt for a command, read input, and echo a normalized command.

chapters.name: Variables, Constants, and Game State
chapters.concept: Variables, constants, assignment, and scope
chapters.deliverable: Track player position, heading, running state, and immutable map dimensions.

chapters.name: Constrain Coordinates and Angles
chapters.concept: Scalar types, subtypes, ranges, and conversions
chapters.deliverable: Replace raw Integer values with coordinate, angle, row, and column subtypes that reject invalid states.

chapters.name: Model Directions and Tiles
chapters.concept: Enumeration types, attributes, and case statements
chapters.deliverable: Represent directions, commands, and tile kinds with enumerations instead of strings and integers.

chapters.name: Build the Main Game Loop
chapters.concept: loop, while, for, exit, and iteration invariants
chapters.deliverable: Create a stable input-update-render loop that exits only through a clear quit condition.

chapters.name: Move Behavior into Procedures
chapters.concept: Procedures and in/out/in out modes
chapters.deliverable: Extract prompting, movement, turning, and status display into procedures with deliberate parameter modes.

chapters.name: Compute Instead of Mutate
chapters.concept: Functions, return types, expression functions, and purity
chapters.deliverable: Implement functions for next position, normalized angle, tile lookup, and command parsing.

chapters.name: Separate the Dungeon into Packages
chapters.concept: Package specifications, bodies, visibility, with, and use type
chapters.deliverable: Create clear packages for types, maps, player state, math, input, rendering, and the game loop.

chapters.name: Represent the Player and Camera
chapters.concept: Records, aggregates, defaults, and nested records
chapters.deliverable: Replace loose variables with Player_State, Camera_State, and Position records.

chapters.name: Create the Map Grid
chapters.concept: Constrained and unconstrained arrays
chapters.deliverable: Represent rooms and corridors as a two-dimensional tile array with explicit row and column ranges.

chapters.name: Protect Map Invariants
chapters.concept: Private types, constructors, selectors, and invariants
chapters.deliverable: Wrap the raw grid in a Map type that guarantees solid outer walls and exactly one spawn point.

chapters.name: Parse Commands Reliably
chapters.concept: String slices, fixed strings, unbounded strings, and character handling
chapters.deliverable: Build a command parser that trims whitespace, normalizes case, and reports useful errors.

chapters.name: Contain Errors at Boundaries
chapters.concept: Exception declarations, handlers, propagation, and cleanup
chapters.deliverable: Define domain-specific map and command errors while keeping the main loop alive after recoverable failures.

chapters.name: Load Maps from Disk
chapters.concept: Text file input/output and resource management
chapters.deliverable: Read a map file, preserve line structure, and close files correctly on success and failure.

chapters.name: Validate the Map Format
chapters.concept: Parsing, validation passes, and error reporting
chapters.deliverable: Parse headers, dimensions, tiles, player spawn, doors, and comments into the Map model.

chapters.name: Add Vector Mathematics
chapters.concept: Floating-point types, vectors, operators, and numeric packages
chapters.deliverable: Introduce 2D vectors for player position, direction, and camera plane while preserving map coordinates.

chapters.name: Turn the Camera
chapters.concept: Ada.Numerics, trigonometry, modular angles, and precision
chapters.deliverable: Rotate direction and camera vectors using sine and cosine with a clearly documented angle unit.

chapters.name: Generate One Ray per Screen Column
chapters.concept: Interpolation, coordinate transforms, and functions over ranges
chapters.deliverable: Convert a screen column into a camera-space X coordinate and a world-space ray direction.

chapters.name: Find Walls with DDA
chapters.concept: Algorithmic loops, step selection, and termination
chapters.deliverable: Implement grid-based digital differential analysis to find the first wall hit by each ray.

chapters.name: Render the Pseudo-3D View
chapters.concept: Formatting, nested loops, buffers, and separation of model/view
chapters.deliverable: Convert ray distances into wall heights and render a buffered ASCII or ANSI frame with ceiling, walls, and floor.

chapters.name: Move Through the Dungeon Safely
chapters.concept: State transitions, collision detection, and boundary checks
chapters.deliverable: Apply forward, backward, and strafing movement only when the destination is walkable.

chapters.name: Doors, Pickups, and Save Data
chapters.concept: Variant records, collections, and persistence
chapters.deliverable: Add simple doors and pickups plus save/load of player state and changed map state.

chapters.name: Finish Dungeon Walk
chapters.concept: AUnit, assertions, documentation, profiling, and release discipline
chapters.deliverable: Complete tests, clean package boundaries, warning-free builds, user instructions, sample maps, and a tagged release.

projects.name: Project 2 — Mission Systems Simulator
projects.goal: Build a deterministic avionics-style mission simulator with typed messages, scheduled components, concurrency, fault injection, networking, and tests.
projects.final_product: A deterministic multi-component simulation with contracts, tasking, protected state, serialization, external interfaces, fault scenarios, and engineering documentation.

chapters.name: Define the Simulator Architecture
chapters.concept: Layered architecture and GPR/Alire organization
chapters.deliverable: Create domain, runtime, adapters, and application layers with dependency rules.

chapters.name: Create Strong Physical Units
chapters.concept: Derived numeric types, private types, and unit-safe APIs
chapters.deliverable: Model altitude, speed, heading, time, voltage, and fuel as incompatible types.

chapters.name: Model Bus Messages
chapters.concept: Discriminated records and variant parts
chapters.deliverable: Represent command, status, sensor, and fault messages with one validated message family.

chapters.name: Define Replaceable Components
chapters.concept: Tagged types, interfaces, dispatching, and composition
chapters.deliverable: Define component and sensor interfaces with initialization, step, status, and shutdown operations.

chapters.name: Build Reusable Typed Utilities
chapters.concept: Generic packages and formal parameters
chapters.deliverable: Create generic bounded buffers, value filters, and range monitors for strongly typed values.

chapters.name: Manage Components and Events
chapters.concept: Ada.Containers vectors, maps, sets, and iteration
chapters.deliverable: Store registered components, scheduled events, active faults, and telemetry history in appropriate containers.

chapters.name: Control Dynamic Lifetime
chapters.concept: Access types, accessibility, aliased objects, and ownership boundaries
chapters.deliverable: Use access values only where dynamic polymorphism or lifetime requires them and document the owner.

chapters.name: Manage Resources Safely
chapters.concept: Controlled and limited controlled types
chapters.deliverable: Wrap a log stream or external handle so initialization and finalization are reliable during exceptions.

chapters.name: State Contracts Explicitly
chapters.concept: Preconditions, postconditions, predicates, invariants, and assertions
chapters.deliverable: Add contracts to units, messages, scheduler operations, and component state transitions.

chapters.name: Design Error and Logging Boundaries
chapters.concept: Exception taxonomy, logging levels, and fault containment
chapters.deliverable: Separate configuration errors, data errors, component faults, and fatal runtime failures.

chapters.name: Load Typed Configuration
chapters.concept: Configuration parsing, defaults, validation, and immutable startup state
chapters.deliverable: Load tick rate, component definitions, routes, and fault scenarios into validated records.

chapters.name: Serialize Messages and State
chapters.concept: Streams, attributes, representation, and versioned formats
chapters.deliverable: Serialize bus messages, snapshots, and replay records with an explicit format version.

chapters.name: Introduce Active Components
chapters.concept: Tasks, task types, activation, termination, and ownership
chapters.deliverable: Run selected sensors or adapters as tasks while keeping the deterministic domain step explicit.

chapters.name: Protect Shared Runtime State
chapters.concept: Protected objects, protected functions, procedures, and entries
chapters.deliverable: Create protected telemetry snapshots and bounded message queues.

chapters.name: Coordinate with Entries and Select
chapters.concept: Rendezvous, entry guards, selective accept, and asynchronous transfer concepts
chapters.deliverable: Build a control task that accepts pause, resume, step, snapshot, and shutdown requests.

chapters.name: Use Real-Time Timing Correctly
chapters.concept: Ada.Real_Time, Time_Span, delay until, priorities, and drift
chapters.deliverable: Drive wall-clock demonstrations with delay until while measuring deadline misses.

chapters.name: Build a Deterministic Scheduler
chapters.concept: Logical time, ordering, fixed steps, and reproducibility
chapters.deliverable: Advance all components in a stable order using integer simulation ticks independent of wall time.

chapters.name: Schedule Events and Inject Faults
chapters.concept: Priority queues, state machines, and scenario execution
chapters.deliverable: Schedule timed commands, sensor failures, stale data, dropouts, and recovery events.

chapters.name: Expose a Network Adapter
chapters.concept: Sockets, framing, partial reads, timeouts, and adapter isolation
chapters.deliverable: Send telemetry and receive commands over a simple framed TCP or UDP protocol.

chapters.name: Wrap a C Interface Safely
chapters.concept: Interfaces.C, Convention, import/export, pointers, and representation clauses
chapters.deliverable: Call a small C checksum or timing function through a narrow Ada wrapper.

chapters.name: Test and Analyze the Simulator
chapters.concept: AUnit, property-style tests, SPARK boundaries, and static analysis
chapters.deliverable: Build unit, integration, replay, concurrency, and contract tests for the deterministic core.

chapters.name: Profile and Release Mission Sim
chapters.concept: Performance measurement, diagnostics, compatibility, and release engineering
chapters.deliverable: Profile the scheduler, bound memory growth, document APIs, freeze a scenario format, and tag a release.

projects.name: Project 3 — Rescue Run SDL
projects.goal: Build a compact top-down rescue game using SDLada and the map ideas learned in Dungeon Walk.
projects.final_product: A polished top-down game with maps, camera, animation, enemies, objectives, audio, configuration, tests, and a release build.

chapters.name: Open an SDL Window
chapters.concept: SDLada dependency and lifecycle
chapters.deliverable: Add SDLada through Alire, initialize SDL, create a window, and shut down safely.

chapters.name: Draw Basic Shapes
chapters.concept: Renderers, colors, coordinates, and resource wrappers
chapters.deliverable: Create a renderer and draw the player, walls, and background using primitives.

chapters.name: Process SDL Events
chapters.concept: Event unions, polling loops, and quit behavior
chapters.deliverable: Handle window close, keyboard, focus, and resize events without blocking rendering.

chapters.name: Create an Input Abstraction
chapters.concept: State versus events and command mapping
chapters.deliverable: Translate keyboard state into game commands independent of SDL scancodes.

chapters.name: Use a Fixed Update Step
chapters.concept: Frame time, accumulator loops, and interpolation
chapters.deliverable: Separate variable rendering frequency from a fixed game update rate.

chapters.name: Move the Player
chapters.concept: Vectors, velocity, acceleration, and input response
chapters.deliverable: Implement responsive top-down movement with normalized diagonal speed.

chapters.name: Reuse the Map Format
chapters.concept: Data adapters and shared format concepts
chapters.deliverable: Load the Dungeon Walk map format into an SDL-oriented world model.

chapters.name: Render Tile Maps
chapters.concept: Nested iteration, clipping, and tile lookup
chapters.deliverable: Draw only visible floor, wall, hazard, and exit tiles.

chapters.name: Follow the Player with a Camera
chapters.concept: World-to-screen transforms and clamping
chapters.deliverable: Implement a smooth camera that follows the player and remains inside map bounds.

chapters.name: Resolve Tile Collision
chapters.concept: Bounding boxes, swept movement, and axis separation
chapters.deliverable: Prevent the player from crossing walls while allowing smooth sliding.

chapters.name: Load and Manage Textures
chapters.concept: SDL_image, ownership, caching, and failure fallback
chapters.deliverable: Load a tileset and sprites through a texture cache with explicit ownership.

chapters.name: Animate the Player
chapters.concept: Sprite sheets, frame timing, and state machines
chapters.deliverable: Select idle, walk, and hurt animations based on player state and direction.

chapters.name: Add Enemy Behavior
chapters.concept: Finite-state machines and steering
chapters.deliverable: Create enemies that idle, detect, chase, search, and return.

chapters.name: Add Attacks and Projectiles
chapters.concept: Object lifetime, collision queries, and cooldowns
chapters.deliverable: Create projectiles or short-range attacks with owner, velocity, lifetime, and damage.

chapters.name: Build Rescue Objectives
chapters.concept: Collections, triggers, and game rules
chapters.deliverable: Add survivors, pickups, exits, and a win condition based on completed rescues.

chapters.name: Create a HUD
chapters.concept: SDL_ttf, formatting, and view models
chapters.deliverable: Display health, rescued count, objective status, and pause state.

chapters.name: Add Sound and Music
chapters.concept: SDL_mixer, channels, resource policy, and events
chapters.deliverable: Play movement, rescue, damage, attack, and victory audio from game events.

chapters.name: Organize Game States
chapters.concept: State pattern, pause, menus, and transitions
chapters.deliverable: Separate title, playing, paused, victory, and game-over states.

chapters.name: Load Settings and Save Progress
chapters.concept: Configuration, save data, and compatibility
chapters.deliverable: Persist controls, volume, window settings, best score, and unlocked maps.

chapters.name: Finish Rescue Run SDL
chapters.concept: Integration tests, performance, packaging, and release polish
chapters.deliverable: Complete maps, balance, tests, warning cleanup, asset licensing notes, and release instructions.

projects.name: Project 4 — Rescue Run ECS Conversion
projects.goal: Rebuild the SDL game in controlled stages as a data-oriented ECS while preserving behavior.
projects.final_product: An ECS-based version of Rescue Run with deterministic systems, data-driven entities, tests, profiling, and a documented migration.

chapters.name: Plan the ECS Migration
chapters.concept: Architecture assessment and behavior baselines
chapters.deliverable: Inventory current entities, data, behavior, coupling, and frame phases before changing code.

chapters.name: Create Stable Entity IDs
chapters.concept: Opaque identifiers, generations, and lifecycle
chapters.deliverable: Implement entity creation, destruction, validity checks, and ID reuse protection.

chapters.name: Define Plain Components
chapters.concept: Data-oriented records and component boundaries
chapters.deliverable: Create position, velocity, sprite, collider, health, AI, projectile, and objective components.

chapters.name: Build Component Stores
chapters.concept: Generics, sparse/dense storage, and membership
chapters.deliverable: Create reusable typed stores supporting add, remove, get, contains, and iteration.

chapters.name: Create the World API
chapters.concept: Encapsulation, composition, and entity construction
chapters.deliverable: Combine entity management and component stores behind a coherent World package.

chapters.name: Define System Phases
chapters.concept: System interfaces, ordering, and dependencies
chapters.deliverable: Create input, movement, collision, combat, AI, animation, objective, cleanup, and rendering phases.

chapters.name: Query Matching Entities
chapters.concept: Component signatures and iteration joins
chapters.deliverable: Iterate only entities containing each system’s required component set.

chapters.name: Defer Structural Changes
chapters.concept: Command buffers and safe mutation phases
chapters.deliverable: Queue spawn, destroy, add, and remove operations until a defined synchronization point.

chapters.name: Create an ECS Event Stream
chapters.concept: Typed events, buffering, and decoupling
chapters.deliverable: Publish collision, damage, rescue, sound, animation, and state-transition events.

chapters.name: Migrate Movement
chapters.concept: Pure systems and component transforms
chapters.deliverable: Move player, enemies, and projectiles using position and velocity components.

chapters.name: Migrate Rendering
chapters.concept: Read-only systems and adapter boundaries
chapters.deliverable: Render entities possessing transform and sprite components through the SDL adapter.

chapters.name: Migrate Collision
chapters.concept: Broad phase, narrow phase, and event generation
chapters.deliverable: Detect tile and entity collisions from collider and transform components.

chapters.name: Migrate Animation
chapters.concept: Component state and time-driven systems
chapters.deliverable: Advance animation components and derive clips from movement or action state.

chapters.name: Migrate Enemy AI
chapters.concept: State components, decision systems, and navigation data
chapters.deliverable: Represent AI state as components and update decisions separately from movement.

chapters.name: Serialize ECS Worlds
chapters.concept: Stable IDs, component schemas, and versioning
chapters.deliverable: Save selected components and reconstruct entities without persisting runtime-only resources.

chapters.name: Measure Data-Oriented Performance
chapters.concept: Profiling, cache behavior, allocations, and complexity
chapters.deliverable: Measure system time, entity counts, store density, allocations, and query cost.

chapters.name: Test and Debug the ECS
chapters.concept: System isolation, world fixtures, invariants, and debug views
chapters.deliverable: Build focused system tests, lifecycle tests, schedule tests, and a component-inspection overlay.

chapters.name: Complete the ECS Conversion
chapters.concept: Migration completion, architecture review, and release comparison
chapters.deliverable: Reach feature parity, remove obsolete object-oriented paths, document tradeoffs, and tag the ECS release.

projects.name: Project 5 — Forge Map Editor
projects.goal: Build a useful GtkAda map editor for the Dungeon Walk and Rescue Run map formats, inspired by proven map-editor workflows without copying a full professional editor.
projects.final_product: A desktop editor with a central map canvas, palette, layers, objects, properties, shortcuts, undo/redo, validation, autosave, and export.

chapters.name: Create the GtkAda Application
chapters.concept: GtkAda dependency, initialization, application/window lifecycle
chapters.deliverable: Add GtkAda through Alire and open a main application window with safe startup and shutdown.

chapters.name: Build the Editor Shell
chapters.concept: Hierarchical widget composition and containers
chapters.deliverable: Create a Tiled-like but simpler shell: central canvas, left palette, right properties/layers, bottom status area.

chapters.name: Menus, Actions, and Shortcuts
chapters.concept: Gtk actions, menus, accelerators, and command routing
chapters.deliverable: Add New, Open, Save, Save As, Undo, Redo, Validate, Export, and Quit actions.

chapters.name: Separate the Document Model
chapters.concept: Model-view separation, observable state, and domain APIs
chapters.deliverable: Create an editor document independent of Gtk widgets, containing map, layers, objects, selection, and dirty state.

chapters.name: Draw the Map Canvas
chapters.concept: Gtk.Drawing_Area, Cairo, expose/draw callbacks, and coordinate transforms
chapters.deliverable: Render tiles, grid, spawn, objects, selection, and viewport through a drawing area.

chapters.name: Pan, Zoom, and Pointer Input
chapters.concept: Signals, event masks, gestures, and viewport math
chapters.deliverable: Support wheel zoom, middle-button or space-drag pan, cursor position, and coordinate conversion.

chapters.name: Create the Tile Palette
chapters.concept: Lists, icon views, selection models, and reusable widgets
chapters.deliverable: Show available tile types with names, icons/colors, and the current brush.

chapters.name: Implement Map Painting Tools
chapters.concept: Mouse signals, tools, drag operations, and model updates
chapters.deliverable: Add pencil, rectangle fill, flood fill, erase, and eyedropper tools.

chapters.name: Add Layers
chapters.concept: Tree/list models, visibility, locking, ordering, and composition
chapters.deliverable: Support tile, object, and trigger layers with add, delete, rename, reorder, visibility, and lock.

chapters.name: Place Objects and Triggers
chapters.concept: Object models, shapes, hit testing, and custom data
chapters.deliverable: Place spawn points, pickups, enemies, exits, rectangles, points, and trigger regions on object layers.

chapters.name: Select and Manipulate Content
chapters.concept: Selection models, hit testing, drag/resize, and multi-selection
chapters.deliverable: Select tiles or objects, draw selection outlines, move objects, and delete selected content.

chapters.name: Build the Properties Inspector
chapters.concept: Forms, widget binding, validation, and context-sensitive panels
chapters.deliverable: Display and edit properties for map, layer, tile selection, or object selection.

chapters.name: Implement Undo and Redo
chapters.concept: Command pattern, reversible changes, stacks, and grouping
chapters.deliverable: Represent painting, object movement, property changes, and layer edits as reversible commands.

chapters.name: Open, Save, and Export
chapters.concept: Gtk file dialogs, recent files, serialization, and atomic writes
chapters.deliverable: Open and save editor documents plus export compatible map files for the games.

chapters.name: Validate Maps and Show Errors
chapters.concept: Dialogs, list views, diagnostics, and navigation to errors
chapters.deliverable: Detect invalid borders, missing spawn, unreachable exits, bad properties, and overlapping critical objects.

chapters.name: Autosave and Background Work
chapters.concept: Gtk main loop, tasks, synchronization, and idle callbacks
chapters.deliverable: Autosave snapshots and run expensive validation without freezing the UI.

chapters.name: Custom Widgets and Visual Polish
chapters.concept: Widget composition, Gtk.Builder, CSS themes, and reusable controls
chapters.deliverable: Create at least one reusable custom composite widget and load part of the UI through Gtk.Builder or Glade XML.

chapters.name: Finish Forge Map Editor
chapters.concept: End-to-end integration, usability testing, packaging, and documentation
chapters.deliverable: Complete the editor workflow, sample maps, tests, keyboard reference, recovery behavior, and release packaging.

stages.name: Project 1 — Dungeon Walk
stages.goal: Build a terminal-rendered, Doom-style 3D dungeon that loads maps and lets the player walk through rooms and corridors.

stages.name: Project 2 — Mission Systems Simulator
stages.goal: Build a deterministic avionics-style mission simulator with typed messages, scheduled components, concurrency, fault injection, networking, and tests.

stages.name: Project 3 — Rescue Run SDL
stages.goal: Build a compact top-down rescue game using SDLada and the map ideas learned in Dungeon Walk.

stages.name: Project 4 — Rescue Run ECS Conversion
stages.goal: Rebuild the SDL game in controlled stages as a data-oriented ECS while preserving behavior.

stages.name: Project 5 — Forge Map Editor
stages.goal: Build a useful GtkAda map editor for the Dungeon Walk and Rescue Run map formats, inspired by proven map-editor workflows without copying a full professional editor.

quests.title: Create the Alire Workspace — Question
quests.summary: Create the dungeon_walk crate, understand alire.toml, build it, run it, and locate generated artifacts.
quests.deliverable: Create the dungeon_walk crate, understand alire.toml, build it, run it, and locate generated artifacts.
quests.extension: Add debug and release build profiles plus a repeatable run command.
quests.risk: Running commands outside the crate or editing generated build artifacts instead of source.
quests.prompt: Answer one focused question before editing code: How should Alire crates and build workflow be used to accomplish this milestone: Create the dungeon_walk crate, understand alire.toml, build it, run it, and locate generated artifacts.
instructions: Relate your answer directly to Dungeon Walk, not to an abstract tutorial.
Name at least one design mistake that could cause this failure: Running commands outside the crate or editing generated build artifacts instead of source.
Identify which of these files should own the decision: alire.toml, src/dungeon_walk.adb, README.md.
acceptance: The answer explains the concept in your own words.
The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
rubric: The answer explains the concept in your own words.
The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
A strong answer supports the milestone: Create the dungeon_walk crate, understand alire.toml, build it, run it, and locate generated artifacts.
quests.verification: alr build and alr run both succeed from the crate root.
quests.alire_commands:
cd ~/Documents/src/projects/ada-mastery/dungeon_walk
alr build
alr run

quests.deliverable: Create protected telemetry snapshots and bounded message queues.

quests.risk: Holding protected locks while performing file or network I/O.
quests.prompt: Answer one focused question before editing code: How should Protected objects, protected functions, procedures, and entries be used to accomplish this milestone: Create protected telemetry snapshots and bounded message queues.

Name at least one design mistake that could cause this failure: Holding protected locks while performing file or network I/O.
Identify which of these files should own the decision: src/runtime/protected_queues.ads, src/runtime/telemetry_store.ads.
acceptance: The answer explains the concept in your own words.
The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
rubric: The answer explains the concept in your own words.
The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
A strong answer supports the milestone: Create protected telemetry snapshots and bounded message queues.

The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.

The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
rubric: The answer explains the concept in your own words.
The answer connects the concept to a concrete package or data boundary.
The answer identifies a plausible failure and how the design prevents it.
